blocks:
Ayahs

ٱقْرَأْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ (١)
Read in the Name of your Lord who created; (1)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ (٢)
created man from a clinging mass. (2)
ٱقْرَأْ وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْأَكْرَمُ (٣)
Read, and your Lord is the most generous, (3)
ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَ بِٱلْقَلَمِ (٤)
who taught by the pen, (4)
عَلَّمَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ (٥)
taught man what he did not know. (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Alaq"

The consensus among commentators is that this Surah is the first Surah revealed to the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and its content further supports this view. The claim by some that the first Surah was Surah "Al-Hamd" or Surah "Al-Muddaththir" contradicts the well-known historical position.

At its beginning, this Surah commands the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to read and recite.

It then speaks of the creation of this magnificent human being from a worthless piece of clotted blood.

In the next stage, it discusses the evolution of man under the light of the Lord's grace and generosity, and his introduction to science, knowledge, and the pen.

In the subsequent stage, it speaks of ungrateful humans who, despite all these divine blessings and honors, choose the path of rebellion.

Finally, it refers to the painful punishment of those who hinder people's guidance and righteous deeds.

The Surah concludes with the command of "prostration" (Sajdah) and "nearness" (Taqarrub) to the presence of the Lord.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"Whoever recites 'Iqra' bi-ismi Rabbika' during his day or night, and then dies during that day or night, has died a martyr; Allah will raise him as a martyr, place him in the ranks of the martyrs, and on the Day of Resurrection, he will be like one who fought with his sword in the way of Allah alongside the Messenger of Allah!"

This Surah is named Surah "Al-Alaq," Surah "Iqra'," or Surah "Al-Qalam" due to the various expressions found at its beginning. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 478.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ (1) خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ (2) اقْرَأْ وَ رَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ (3) الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ (4) عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ (5)

Occasion of Revelation

As mentioned in the description of the Surah's content, most commentators believe this Surah is the first to be revealed to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In fact, some state that there is a consensus among all commentators that the above five verses were revealed at the very beginning of the revelation to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and the content itself supports this.

It is mentioned in narrations that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had gone to Mount "Hira" when Gabriel came and said: "O Muhammad, read!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "I am not a reader."

Gabriel embraced him and pressed him, then said again: "Read!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeated the same answer. Gabriel did this a second time and received the same response. On the third time, he said: اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ... (until the end of the five verses).

He said this and disappeared from the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله sight.

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, who was deeply exhausted by receiving the first rays of revelation, came to Khadijah and said: "Zammiluni wa daththiruni" ("Cover me and throw a garment over me so I may rest"). [^1] Al-Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan also narrates that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said to Khadijah: "When I am alone, I hear a call (and I am concerned!)."

Khadijah said: "Allah will do nothing but good for you, for

[^1]: Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 12, p. 96 (slightly summarized) - Many commentators from both schools (Sunni and Shia) have narrated this with more details, some of which are unacceptable.

by Allah, you fulfill trusts, maintain family ties, and are truthful in speech."

Khadijah says: "After this incident, we went to Waraqah ibn Nawfal (he was a learned Arab and Khadijah's cousin). The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله described what he had seen to Waraqah. Waraqah said: 'When that caller comes to you, pay close attention to what you hear, then tell me.'"

In his seclusion, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله heard the caller saying: "O Muhammad, say: بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ until وَ لَا الضَّالِّينَ, and say: لا اله الا اللَّه." Then the Prophet came to Waraqah and recounted the matter to him.

Waraqah said: "Glad tidings to you, and again glad tidings! I bear witness that you are the one whom Jesus, son of Mary, gave glad tidings of! You have a Law (Sharia) like that of Moses; you are a sent Prophet, and soon after this day, you will be commanded to struggle (Jihad). If I live to see that day, I will fight alongside you!"

When Waraqah passed away, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I saw this scholar in Paradise (the Barzakh Paradise) wearing silk garments, for he believed in me and testified to my truth." [^1] Of course, in some words of commentators or history books, discordant accounts regarding this period of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life are seen, which are certainly from fabricated hadiths and Isra'iliyyat (Judeo-Christian traditions). For instance, that the Prophet became very distressed after the first revelation and feared it might be satanic inspirations! Or that he decided several times to throw himself off the mountain! Such nonsense is compatible neither with the lofty station of Prophethood nor with what is recorded in history regarding the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله extraordinary intellect, wisdom, management, patience, self-control, and confidence.

It seems such weak and crude narrations were crafted by the enemies of Islam, intending to question both Islam and the person of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. With this in mind, we turn to the commentary of the verses.

Commentary

Read in the Name of Your Lord

In the first verse, addressing the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Read in the name of your Lord who created [the universe]" (اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ). [^1] Some have said the object in the above sentence is omitted, and originally it was: "Read the Quran in the name of your Lord." For this reason, some have taken this verse as evidence that Bismillah is part of the Surahs of the Quran. [^2] Others have considered the "Ba" (ب) to be extra (superfluous) and said the meaning is "Read the name of your Lord," but this interpretation seems unlikely, as it would be more appropriate to say "Remember the name of your Lord."

It is noteworthy that here, before anything else, emphasis is placed on the "Lordship" (Rububiyyah) of God. We know that "Rabb" means "the Reforming Owner"—one who both owns something and attends to its reform and upbringing.

Then, to prove the Lordship of God, emphasis is placed on the issue of creation and the genesis of the

[^1]: Al-Raghib in Al-Mufradat says: "Qira'ah" (reading) means joining letters and words together; hence, a single letter is not called "Qira'ah." [^2]: In this case, the "Ba" is for "mulabasah" (accompaniment).

universe, because the best evidence for His Lordship is His Creatorship. He who manages the world is its Creator.

This is, in fact, a response to the Arab polytheists who accepted God's Creatorship but attributed Lordship and management to idols! Furthermore, God's Lordship and His management of the system of existence are the best evidence for proving His Holy Essence.

Then, from among all creatures, He emphasizes the most important phenomenon of the world of creation and the pinnacle of existence—namely, "Man"—and reminds us of his creation, saying: "The same God who created man from a clot of blood" (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ).

"Min 'alaq" originally means sticking to something. Therefore, clotted blood and also a "leech" that sticks to the body to suck blood are called "'alaq." Since the embryo, after passing its initial stages in the womb, takes the form of a piece of sticky clotted blood that appears very insignificant, this verse identifies the origin of human creation as this tiny entity, so that the great power of the Lord becomes clear—how He created such a valuable creature from such a worthless thing.

Some have also said: "Alaq" here refers to the clay of Adam, which also had a sticky quality. Clearly, the God who brought this wondrous creature into existence from that piece of "sticky clay" is worthy of all praise.

Sometimes "'alaq" has been interpreted as a "possessor of attachment" (social being), referring to the social spirit of man and their attachment to one another, which in fact forms the primary basis for human evolution and the progress of civilizations.

Some also consider "'alaq" to refer to the "male seed" (sperm), which bears a strong resemblance to a "leech." This microscopic entity swims in the seminal fluid, moves toward the "female egg" in the womb, sticks to it, and from the combination of the two, the complete human embryo is formed.

It is true that at that time such matters were not yet known, but the Holy Quran has unveiled them through scientific miracles.

Among these four interpretations, the first seems the clearest, although combining all four interpretations is also possible.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that "Man" according to one interpretation refers to Prophet Adam, and according to the other three interpretations, it refers to all human beings.

Once again, for emphasis, He adds: "Read, and your Lord is the Most Generous, and the Most Noble of all the noble" (اقْرَأْ وَ رَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ). [^1] Some believe that the second "Iqra'" is an emphasis on the "Iqra'" in the previous verses, while others say it is different: in the first sentence, the meaning is the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله reading for himself, and in the second, it is reading for the people. However, emphasis seems more appropriate, as there is no evidence for this distinction. In any case, the expression in this verse is actually a response to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله words to Gabriel when he said: "I am not a reader," meaning: "By the blessing of your exceptionally Generous and Noble Lord, you have the ability to read and recite."

[^1]: The sentence "wa Rabbuka al-Akram" is an initial nominal sentence composed of a "subject" and "predicate."

Then, He proceeds to describe the God who is the Most Generous of the generous, saying:

"The One who taught by the pen" (الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ).

"And taught man what he did not know" (عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ). In fact, these verses are also a response to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله statement "I am not a reader," meaning: the same God who taught humans by means of the pen and taught man what he did not know is capable of teaching an unlettered servant like you how to read and recite.

The sentence (الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ) carries two meanings: first, that God taught man writing and the book, and created in him the power and ability for this great task, which is the origin of human history and the source of all sciences, arts, and civilizations.

Second, it means that He taught man sciences and knowledge through this path and by this means.

In short, according to one interpretation, the meaning is the teaching of writing, and according to the other, it refers to the sciences that have reached man through writing.

In any case, it is a profound expression reflected in these great and meaningful verses during those sensitive moments of the first revelation.

Key Points

1. The Beginning of Revelation was Accompanied by the Start of a Scientific Movement

These verses, as we have said, are believed by most or all commentators to be the first verses revealed to the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. With the shining of the first rays of revelation, a new chapter began in human history, and mankind became the recipient of one of the greatest divine favors. The most complete of divine religions, which was the final point and seal of religions, was revealed. After the revelation of all Islamic laws and teachings, according to the verse: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَ أَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَ رَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً (Al-Ma'idah: 3), the divine religion was perfected, His blessing reached its peak of completion, and Islam became the religion pleasing to God.

A very interesting point here is that while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was "Ummi" (unlettered) and the environment of Hijaz was entirely engulfed in ignorance, the first verses of revelation emphasize "knowledge" and "the pen," mentioned immediately after the great blessing of creation! In fact, these verses first report the "physical" evolution of man from an insignificant entity like a "clot" ('alaqah), and on the other hand, speak of the "spiritual" evolution through teaching and learning, especially by means of the pen.

On the day these verses were revealed, not only in the environment of Hijaz—which was an environment of ignorance—did no one value the pen, but even in the civilized world of that time, the pen held little importance.

But today we know that all civilizations, sciences, knowledge, and progress achieved by man in every field revolve around the pen. Truly, "the ink of the scholars" has surpassed "the blood of the martyrs," because both the foundation of the martyr's blood and its support are the inks of the scholars' pens. Fundamentally, the fate of human societies is primarily tied to the tips of pens.

The reform of human societies begins with faithful and committed pens, and the corruption and ruin of societies also stem from poisonous and corrupt pens.

It is not without reason that the Holy Quran has sworn by the pen and what they write with it—meaning both the "tool" and the "product" of that tool—where He says: ن وَ الْقَلَمِ وَ ما يَسْطُرُونَ (Al-Qalam: 1).

We know that the period of human life is divided into two eras: The historical era. And the pre-historic era.

The historical era is when the pen, reading, and writing were invented, and man was able to record something of his life with the pen and leave it as a legacy for future generations. Thus, human history is equal to the history of the emergence of the pen and script!

Regarding the role of the pen in human life, we provided a detailed explanation at the beginning of Surah "Al-Qalam" in Volume 24 of Tafsir Nemouneh.

Therefore, the foundation of Islam was laid upon knowledge and the pen from the very beginning. It is not without reason that a people so backward progressed so much in sciences and knowledge that—by the admission of friend and foe alike—they exported knowledge to the whole world. According to famous European historians, it was the light of Muslim knowledge that shone upon the dark face of medieval Europe and brought them into the age of civilization.

In this regard, many books have been written by them under titles such as "The History of Islamic Civilization" or "The Legacy of Islam."

How unsightly it is for such a nation and such a religion to lag behind in the field of science and knowledge and become needy of others, or even dependent on them.

3. Remembrance of God in Every State

The beginning of the Noble Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission started with the mention of God's name: "اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ".

And interestingly, his entire fruitful life was intertwined with the mention and remembrance of God.

The remembrance of God was coupled with his every breath; he would stand, sit, sleep, walk, mount, dismount, and stop—all with the remembrance of God and the name of "Allah."

When he woke up from sleep, he would say: "Alhamdu lillahil-ladhi ahyana ba'da ma amatana wa ilayhin-nushur" (Praise be to Allah who gave us life after He caused us to die, and to Him is the resurrection).

Ibn Abbas says: "One night I slept in his presence. When he woke up, he raised his head toward the sky and recited the last ten verses of Surah Al-Imran: إِنَّ فِي خَلْقِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ... Then he pleaded: Allahumma lakal-hamd Anta nurus-samawati wal-ard wa man fihinna... Allahumma laka aslamtu wa bika amantu wa 'alayka tawakkaltu wa ilayka anabtu..."

When he left the house, he would say: "Bismillah, tawakkaltu 'alallah. Allahumma inni a'udhu bika an adilla aw udalla, aw azilla aw uzalla, aw adhlima aw udhlama, aw ajhala aw yujhala 'alayya."

When he entered the mosque, he would say: "A'udhu billahil-'Adhim wa bi-wajhihil-Karim wa sultanihil-qadim minash-shaytanir-rajim."

When he put on new clothes, he would say: "Allahumma lakal-hamd Anta kasawtanihi, as'aluka khayrahu wa khayra ma suni'a lahu, wa a'udhu bika min sharrihi wa sharri ma suni'a lahu."

And when he returned home, he would say: "Alhamdu lillahil-ladhi kafani wa awani, wal-hamdu lillahil-ladhi at'amani wa saqani."

In this manner, his entire life was kneaded and mixed with the remembrance of God, the name of God, and the request for God's favors. [^1]

[^1]: Fi Dhilal al-Quran, Vol. 8, p. 619 onwards (heavily summarized).

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَيَطْغَىٰٓ (٦)
Indeed man becomes rebellious (6)
أَن رَّءَاهُ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰٓ (٧)
when he considers himself without need. (7)
إِنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ٱلرُّجْعَىٰٓ (٨)
Indeed to your Lord is the return. (8)
أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يَنْهَىٰ (٩)
Tell me, he who forbids (9)
عَبْدًا إِذَا صَلَّىٰٓ (١٠)
a servant when he prays, (10)
أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَانَ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (١١)
tell me, should he be on [true] guidance, (11)
أَوْ أَمَرَ بِٱلتَّقْوَىٰٓ (١٢)
or bid [others] to Godwariness, (12)
أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ (١٣)
tell me, should he call him a liar and turn away (13)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَم بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرَىٰ (١٤)
—does he not know that Allah sees [him]? (14)

Commentary

Do You Not Know That Allah Sees All Your Deeds?

Following the previous verses, which referred to the material and spiritual blessings of the Lord bestowed upon man—the natural consequence of such extensive blessings being man's gratitude and submission to Allah—the verses under discussion state: "It is not the case that Divine blessings always awaken the spirit of gratitude in man; rather, he most certainly transgresses" (كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى‌) [1].

"Because he considers himself self-sufficient and without need" (أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى‌) [2]. This is the dominant nature of most human beings; the nature of those who have not been nurtured in the school of reason and revelation—that when they perceive themselves as self-sufficient, they begin to rebel and transgress.

They neither serve Allah, nor recognize His laws, nor listen to the call of conscience, nor observe truth and justice.

In reality, neither man nor any other creature will ever be truly self-sufficient or without need; rather, all contingent beings are always in need of Allah's grace and blessings. If His grace were cut off for a single moment, they would all perish at that very instant. However, man sometimes erroneously perceives himself as self-sufficient, and the subtle phrasing of the verse points to this very meaning by saying: "he sees himself as self-sufficient," rather than saying: "he becomes self-sufficient."

Some believe that "man" in this verse refers specifically to "Abu Jahl," who rose in opposition from the beginning of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission. However, "man" here certainly has a general meaning, and individuals like Abu Jahl are merely instances of it.

In any case, it appears that the purpose of the verse is to advise the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not to expect people to accept his call quickly; rather, he must prepare himself for the denial and opposition of arrogant transgressors and know that a path full of ups and downs lies ahead of him.

Then, He threatens these arrogant transgressors, saying: "Indeed, to your Lord is the return of all" (إِنَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى‌). And He is the One who will bring the transgressors to account for their deeds.

Fundamentally, just as the return of everything is toward Him, and everyone dies while the heritage of the heavens and the earth remains for His Holy Essence—"And to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth" (Al-Imran: 180)—so too in the beginning, everything originated from Him. Thus, there is no place for man to consider himself self-sufficient, become arrogant, and transgress.

Then, addressing some of the actions of the arrogant transgressors—their prevention of others from following the path of truth and traversing the way of guidance and piety—He adds: "Have you seen him who forbids..." (أَ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى‌).

"A servant when he prays?" (عَبْداً إِذا صَلَّى‌). Is such a person not deserving of Divine punishment and retribution? It is mentioned in the Hadiths that Abu Jahl asked those around him: "Does Muhammad place his face in the dust (for prostration) among you?" They said: "Yes." He said:

"By that which we swear by, if I see him in such a state, I will crush his neck with my foot!" They told him: "Look, he is there busy praying!" Abu Jahl moved forward to press the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) neck under his foot, but as he drew near, he retreated, using his hands as if pushing something away from himself! They asked him: "What is this state we see in you?" He said: "Suddenly, I saw a trench of fire between myself and him, and a terrifying sight, as well as wings!" At this point, the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "By Him in whose hand is my soul, if he had come closer to me, the angels of Allah would have torn his body into pieces and snatched away his limbs one by one!"

It was here that the above verses were revealed [1]. According to these narrations, the above verses were not revealed at the very beginning of the mission (Bi'thah), but rather when the call of Islam had become public. Therefore, some believe that only the first five verses of this Surah were revealed at the start of the mission, and the rest followed after a considerable interval.

However, in any case, this occasion of revelation never limits the broad scope of the verse's meaning.

In the next verse, for further emphasis, He adds: "Have you seen if this praying servant is on the path of guidance..." (أَ رَأَيْتَ إِنْ كانَ عَلَى الْهُدى‌).

"Or enjoins piety?" (أَوْ أَمَرَ بِالتَّقْوى‌). Is it appropriate to forbid him? And can the punishment for such a forbidder be anything other than the fire of Hell?!

"Have you seen if this transgressing person, who prevents the followers of the path of truth from prayer, guidance, and piety—if he denies the truth and turns away, what a painful fate he will have?!" (أَ رَأَيْتَ إِنْ كَذَّبَ وَ تَوَلَّى‌) [2].

"Does he not know that Allah sees all his deeds" and records and preserves them all for reckoning and recompense? (أَ لَمْ يَعْلَمْ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ يَرى‌).

The use of the conditional structure in the above verses indicates that this arrogant transgressor should at least consider the possibility that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is on the path of guidance and that his call is a call toward piety. This very possibility should be enough to stop his transgression.

Therefore, the meaning of these verses is not doubt regarding the guidance and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call to piety, but rather a reference to the subtle point mentioned above.

Some commentators have directed the pronouns in "was" (كان) and "enjoined" (أمر) back to the person who forbids, such as Abu Jahl. Thus, the meaning of the verses would be: "If he were to accept guidance and, instead of forbidding prayer, enjoin piety, how beneficial would it be for him?"

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

Key Point: The Entire Universe is in the Presence of Allah

Paying attention to the reality that every action man performs is in the presence of Allah—and fundamentally, "the entire universe is the presence of Allah"—and that nothing of man's deeds or even intentions is hidden from Him, can have a profound effect on the program of human life and restrain him from wrongdoing, provided that faith in this matter truly takes root in his heart and becomes a definitive conviction.

In a Hadith, we read: "Worship Allah as if you see Him, for if you do not see Him, He certainly sees you."

It is narrated that a person of awakened heart had repented after a sin and was constantly weeping. They asked him: "Why do you cry so much? Do you not know that Allah the Almighty is All-Forgiving?"

He replied: "Yes, He may forgive, but how can I remove this shame and embarrassment that He saw me?!" "Suppose You overlook my sin out of Your grace, What shall I do with the shame of Your having seen what I did?!"

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 515. [^2]: The phrases "Have you seen" (رأيت) in the three verses above, according to many commentators, mean "Tell me." The answers to the conditions mentioned in these verses are omitted, and the implied meaning is: "What will his state, his punishment, and his torment be like?!"

Ayahs

كَلَّا لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ لَنَسْفَعًۢا بِٱلنَّاصِيَةِ (١٥)
No indeed! If he does not cease, We shall seize him by the forelock, (15)
نَاصِيَةٍ كَـٰذِبَةٍ خَاطِئَةٍ (١٦)
a lying, sinful forelock! (16)
فَلْيَدْعُ نَادِيَهُۥ (١٧)
Then let him call out his gang! (17)
سَنَدْعُ ٱلزَّبَانِيَةَ (١٨)
We [too] shall call the keepers of hell. (18)
كَلَّا لَا تُطِعْهُ وَٱسْجُدْ وَٱقْتَرِب ۩ (١٩)
No indeed! Do not obey him, but prostrate and draw near [to Allah]! (19)

Commentary

Prostrate and Draw Near!

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the disbelieving transgressors and their harassment of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the worshippers, these verses place them under a barrage of the severest threats, stating:

"It is not as he imagines" (he thinks he can place his foot on the Prophet's neck during prostration and prevent him from this divine program) (كلا).

"If he does not desist from this ignorance and pride of his, We will surely drag him by the forelock [towards punishment]" (لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ لَنَسْفَعاً بِالنَّاصِيَةِ).

"A lying, sinful forelock!" (ناصِيَةٍ كاذِبَةٍ خاطِئَةٍ). The word Lanasfa’an is derived from the root Saf’ (on the weight of ‘Afw). According to some commentators, it has various meanings: seizing and pulling violently, slapping the face, blackening the face (the three stones used as a base for a pot over a fire are also called Safa’ because they are black and soot-covered), and finally, marking and humiliating [1]. The most appropriate meaning here is the first one, although other meanings are also possible in the context of this verse.

In any case, does this refer to an event in the Hereafter—where the forelocks of people like Abu Jahl will be seized and dragged toward the fire of Hell—or does it manifest in this world, or both? It is not unlikely to be both, and the following narration serves as evidence.

In a narration, we read: When Surah Ar-Rahman was revealed, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked his companions: "Who among you will recite this Surah to the leaders of the Quraysh?"

Those present remained silent for a moment, fearing the harassment of the Quraysh leaders.

Abdullah ibn Mas'ud stood up and said: "O Messenger of Allah! I will do this..."

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 32, p. 23.

Ibn Mas'ud, who had a small stature and was physically weak, stood up and went to the leaders of the Quraysh. He found them gathered around the Kaaba and began reciting Surah Ar-Rahman.

Abu Jahl stood up and slapped him so hard that his ear was torn and blood began to flow! Ibn Mas'ud came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) weeping. When the Prophet's eyes fell upon him, He became distressed, lowered His head, and fell into deep sorrow.

Suddenly, Gabriel descended while smiling and joyful. The Prophet asked: "O Gabriel, why do you laugh while Ibn Mas'ud is weeping?" He replied: "You will soon know the reason."

This event passed until the Muslims were victorious on the day of the Battle of Badr. Ibn Mas'ud was walking among the fallen polytheists when his eyes fell upon Abu Jahl, who was taking his final breaths. Ibn Mas'ud sat upon his chest. When Abu Jahl saw him, he said: "O lowly shepherd! You have climbed to a high place!" Ibn Mas'ud replied: "Al-Islamu ya'lu wa la yu'la 'alayh":

"Islam is supreme and nothing shall surpass it."

Abu Jahl said to him: "Tell your friend Muhammad: No one in life was more hated by me than Him, even in my death!" When this reached the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He said: "The Pharaoh of my time was worse than the Pharaoh of Moses, for the latter said in his final moments, 'I believe,' but this one's rebellion only increased!" Then Abu Jahl turned to Ibn Mas'ud and said: "Cut off my head with this sword, for it is sharper." When Ibn Mas'ud severed his head, he could not carry it to the Messenger of Allah (he grabbed the hair of his forelock and dragged it across the ground to the Prophet, and thus the meaning of the verse was realized in this world as well) [1]. Nasiyah refers to the hair of the forelock, and "seizing the forelock" is said when one intends to lead someone toward something with humiliation and disgrace, because when the forelock is seized, the person loses all power of movement and has no choice but to submit.

Of course, the word Nasiyah is used both for individuals and for precious objects, just as in Persian we use expressions like "the forehead of the crowd" or "the forehead of the building."

The description "ناصِيَةٍ كاذِبَةٍ خاطِئَةٍ" (a lying, sinful forelock) refers to the person who possesses this forelock, who was both a liar and a sinner, such as Abu Jahl.

In a narration from Ibn Abbas, it is stated that one day Abu Jahl came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) while He was praying near the Station of Abraham (Maqam Ibrahim). He shouted: "Did I not forbid you from this?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) shouted back at him and drove him away. Abu Jahl said: "O Muhammad! Do you shout at me and drive me away? Do you not know that my tribe and kinsmen in this land are more numerous than anyone else's?" [2]. At this point, the next verse was revealed: "Let this arrogant ignorant one call all his tribe and kinsmen and seek help from them" (فَلْيَدْعُ نادِيَهُ).

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 32, p. 23 (summarized). [2]: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 10, p. 624.

"We too shall call the guards of Hell" (سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ). So that it may become clear that this heedless, unaware person can achieve nothing, and in the clutches of the agents of punishment, he is like a piece of straw in the middle of a violent storm! Nadi is derived from Nida (calling) and means a public assembly; sometimes a recreation center is also called Nadi because people call out to one another there.

Some have said it is derived from Nida meaning "generosity," because they entertain one another there. Dar al-Nadwa, which was the name of the famous consultative assembly of the Quraysh, is also derived from this meaning.

However, here Nadi refers to the group gathered in that assembly, or in other words, the tribe, kinsmen, and friends upon whose strength people like Abu Jahl relied in their affairs.

Zabaniyah is the plural of Zabniyah (with a kasra on the za). It originally means "security officers," from the root Zabn (on the weight of Matn) meaning to push back, to strike, and to drive away. Here, it refers to the angels of punishment and the guards of Hell.

In the final verse of this Surah, which is a verse of prostration (Sajdah), He says: "It is not as he imagines, insisting that you abandon prostration" (كلا). "Never obey him! Prostrate to your Lord and draw near to Him" (لا تُطِعْهُ وَ اسْجُدْ وَ اقْتَرِبْ).

The likes of Abu Jahl are too insignificant to prevent your prostration or to throw stones and create obstacles in the path of the progress of your religion. With reliance on your Lord, through supplication, worship, and prostration, walk this path and become closer and closer to your God every day.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that "prostration" causes man's proximity to the threshold of Allah. Hence, in a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that He said: Aqrabu ma yakunu al-'abdu min Allah idha kana sajidan: "The closest a servant is to Allah is when he is in prostration."

Of course, we know that according to the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), there are four obligatory prostrations in the Quran: in Surah Alif Lam Mim As-Sajdah, Fussilat, An-Najm, and here (Surah Al-Alaq); the rest of the prostrations in the Quran are recommended (Mustahabb).

Key Point: Rebellion and the Feeling of Self-Sufficiency

Most of the world's corruptions stem from the affluent and arrogant classes, and they were always in the front line of opposition against the Prophets. The Quran sometimes refers to them as Mala' (the elite - Al-A'raf: 60), sometimes as Mutrafin (those living in luxury - Saba: 34), and sometimes as Mustakbirin (the arrogant - Al-Mu'minun: 67). The first refers to the aristocratic population whose appearance fills the eyes but whose insides are empty; the second refers to those who live in luxury and are intoxicated and proud, unaware of the pain and suffering of others; and the third refers to those who ride the mount of pride and arrogance, distant from God and the people.

The source of all this is the feeling of self-sufficiency and wealth. This is a characteristic of

people with little capacity; when they attain a blessing, wealth, or position, they become so intoxicated and feel so self-sufficient that they even consign God to oblivion.

Yet we know that the book of days can be overturned by a single breeze, and all of a person's wealth can be destroyed in less than an hour—or a flood, earthquake, or lightning bolt can blow it all away. Even his health can be so endangered by a single sip of water getting stuck in his throat that he sees death with his own eyes.

What heedlessness is this that grips a group, making them imagine themselves self-sufficient, as they ride the rebellious mount of pride and make the social arena their playground?

We seek refuge in Allah from such ignorance and folly, and from such unawareness and stubbornness! To eliminate such a state, it is enough for man to reflect a little on his own immeasurable weakness and the great power of the Lord, and to turn the pages of history to see the fate of nations who were stronger and more powerful than him, so that he may dismount from the horse of pride.

O Allah! Protect us from pride and arrogance, which are the primary factors in distancing ourselves from You. O Lord! Do not leave us to ourselves for even a moment in this world or the Hereafter.

O God! Grant us such strength that we may rub the noses of these arrogant oppressors—who block Your path—into the dust and turn their plans into water.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Alaq.
